prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 31-year-old man.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a cold last week (sore throat and congestion) but I still feel more tired than usual - my energy hasn't fully bounced back. No current
 fever.
 - History: No autoimmune disease and not on any regular medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/68, HR 70, Temp 36.7°C
 - Exam: Normal exam at my visit.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 3.3 K/uL (4.0-11.0) low
 - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): 1.5 K/uL (1.5-7.0) borderline
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - Platelets: 220 K/uL (150-400) normal
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's
 here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results show a low white blood cell (WBC) count. This is a common and usually temporary finding after a viral illness like the cold you had, as your body uses these cells to fight the infection. Your neutrophil count (ANC), a key type of white blood cell, is at the very bottom of the normal range but is not at a level considered dangerously low. Your other blood counts, like hemoglobin (for oxygen) and platelets (for clotting), are normal. The lingering fatigue you're feeling is also very common as your body recovers from an infection. This pattern is most often temporary, and the counts typically return to normal on their own over a few weeks as you fully recover.

TRIAGE:
B

CONFIDENCE:
95%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results show a low white blood cell (WBC) count. It is very common for a recent viral infection, like the cold you had, to temporarily cause this. Your body uses these cells to fight the virus, and their numbers can dip during or after the illness. Your neutrophil count, a key type of WBC, is at the very bottom of the normal range but not at a level considered dangerous. Your red blood cells and platelets are normal, which is reassuring. The lingering fatigue you are feeling is also a very common part of recovering from a virus. In most cases, the WBC count will return to normal on its own over the next few weeks as your energy improves.

TRIAGE: B
CONFIDENCE: 95%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results show a low white blood cell (WBC) count. White blood cells are your body's infection-fighting cells. It is very common for a recent viral illness, like the cold you had, to temporarily cause a drop in these cells as your immune system works to clear the virus. This is usually a temporary finding. Your neutrophil count, which is a key type of white blood cell, is at the very bottom of the normal range, which is reassuring. Your other blood counts for red blood cells (hemoglobin) and clotting (platelets) are normal. The lingering fatigue is also very common after a viral infection. The lab results most likely reflect a temporary, post-viral change that will resolve as you continue to recover.

TRIAGE: B
CONFIDENCE: 95%

Your body's immune system used up many of these cells to fight the virus, and it can take some time to build them back up. Your fatigue is also a normal part of this recovery process. The specific type of white blood cell, your neutrophils, is at the very low end of the normal range but not at a level typically considered dangerous. Your red blood cells (hemoglobin) and platelets are normal, which is reassuring. The most likely scenario is that your blood counts will return to normal as you continue to recover over the next few weeks.
